title: Bottom Drawers
intro: Bottom drawers slide up from the page footer and run the full viewport width — the editing surface for grid-anchored workflows. Three viewport-height tiers, one header recipe, and a sticky footer make every drawer identical.

blocks.title: The Laws of Bottom Drawers
blocks.intro: Eight rules govern every bottom drawer. Follow all of them and any two drawers in the product are interchangeable.

items.rule: Heights come from the tier system — compact 50vh, standard 70vh, full 85vh. Never invent a fourth value.
items.why: Viewport-height units keep drawers proportional on every screen; arbitrary px or % heights make sibling drawers visibly inconsistent.

items.rule: Pick the smallest tier that fits the content without scrolling the primary action area.
items.why: Every extra vh hides the grid context the user is editing against. Upgrade a tier only when the content genuinely needs it.

items.rule: Every drawer is three zones: custom header, scrollable body (`flex: 1`, `overflow-y: auto`), sticky footer.
items.why: Only the body scrolls — the title and commit actions stay visible no matter how long the content gets.

items.rule: Disable antd's default chrome — `title={null}`, `closable={false}`, body padding 0 — and build the header yourself.
items.why: antd's built-in header renders differently from the house pattern; nulling it is the only way every drawer ships the same header.

items.rule: Header: padding `12px 24px`, `Texto` h5 title on the left, `CloseOutlined` on the right, 1px bottom border.
items.why: One header recipe means users find the title and the close affordance in the same place in every drawer.

items.rule: Footer: sticky, padding `12px 24px`, 1px top border, actions right-aligned with an 8px gap — Cancel before the primary.
items.why: Sticky placement keeps commit actions reachable while the body scrolls; fixed ordering means muscle memory transfers between drawers.

items.rule: An open drawer must never push the grid below usability — cumulative non-grid chrome stays at or under 320px.
items.why: A drawer that shoves the grid offscreen forces users to close it just to see the result of their edit. Budget the chrome or use overlay placement.

items.rule: Use the antd v5 API: `open`, `afterOpenChange`, `destroyOnHidden` — never `visible`, `afterVisibleChange`, or `destroyOnClose`. Drawer's close handler is `onClose` in both versions.
items.why: The old names survive only as deprecated shims — they warn in console today and disappear on a future antd upgrade.

blocks.title: Size tiers
blocks.caption: The three height tiers — compact 50vh, standard 70vh, full 85vh — with the workloads each is sized for. Choose the smallest tier that fits without scrolling the primary action area.

blocks.title: Drawer anatomy
blocks.caption: Three-zone anatomy — custom header, scrollable body, sticky footer — plus the four props that disable antd's default chrome so the custom header can take over.

blocks.title: Header pattern
blocks.caption: The custom header spec: 12px vertical and 24px horizontal padding, Texto h5 title left, CloseOutlined right, 1px bottom border separating header from body.

blocks.title: Tier and chrome discipline
blocks.caption: Tier discipline (three vh values, nothing else) and chrome nulling (one header recipe) shown side by side with the violations they replace.

blocks.title: When to reach for a bottom drawer
blocks.body:
Bottom drawers are the grid's editing surface. They run the full viewport width, so they fit wide forms, tab sets, and embedded grids that a 400-520px side drawer cannot. Use one when the user edits something anchored to a row while the column context stays visible above — scenario editors, exception drawers, profile managers.

Use a side drawer for narrow inspection and detail panes, and a modal for blocking confirmations. If the editor needs the entire screen, it is a page, not a drawer.

blocks.title: Sizing and spacing tokens
blocks.intro: Three height tokens plus the fixed chrome constants. These are the only dimensional decisions a bottom drawer is allowed to make.

rows.use: Compact tier — single form, confirmation, quick-edit panels
Standard tier — scenario and formula editors, multi-field forms, tabbed content
Full tier — split views, grid + detail layouts, profile managers
Vertical and horizontal padding on the header bar and sticky footer
Gap between right-aligned footer buttons; Cancel sits left of the primary
border-bottom under the header, border-top above the footer
Hard ceiling on cumulative non-grid chrome while a drawer is open against a grid

blocks.title: Canonical bottom drawer
blocks.code:
{/* Header — custom bar replaces antd chrome */} Edit Scenario {/* Body — the only zone that scrolls */}
{children}
{/* Footer — sticky, actions right-aligned */}
blocks.note: This exact skeleton is the starting point for every bottom drawer. Note the v5 props (open, destroyOnHidden), GraviButton's buttonText prop, and theme1 for the primary action — not type='primary'.

items.do: Pull heights from the three tiers: height="50vh", "70vh", or "85vh".
items.dont: Mix units or invent values — height="70%", "80vh", "720px".
items.why: The tier system is what makes sibling drawers feel like one product; arbitrary heights drift immediately.

items.do: Null the default chrome (title={null}, closable={false}) and render the standard custom header bar.
items.dont: Pass a string or node to antd's title prop, or leave closable={true} alongside a custom close icon.
items.why: Mixing antd chrome with custom headers produces three different-looking drawers on one screen — and sometimes two close buttons.

items.do: Keep Cancel and the primary action in the sticky footer, right-aligned, 8px apart.
items.dont: Put commit actions in the header or let them scroll away inside the body.
items.why: Users commit from the same corner in every drawer; scrolling to find Save is a defect, not a style choice.

items.title: Drawer chrome can bury the grid
items.detail: When a drawer stays open while the user works against a grid, every pixel of non-grid chrome counts. Keep the cumulative total — page header, control bar, drawer header and footer, notices — at or under 320px. If even the compact tier buries the grid, switch to overlay placement or rethink the surface; never ship a drawer the user must close to see the result of their own edit.

items.title: Deprecated antd prop names still compile
items.detail: visible, destroyOnClose, and afterVisibleChange are deprecated names. Use open, destroyOnHidden, and afterOpenChange — the deprecated names still compile and emit console warnings today, and disappear on a future antd upgrade. Drawer has no onOpenChange; wire close through onClose.

items.title: GraviButton takes buttonText, not children
items.detail: GraviButton's contract is the buttonText prop. Excalibrr 4.x drops React children silently — <GraviButton>Save</GraviButton> renders an empty button; 5.x falls back to children, but buttonText always wins when both are set. Self-close the element and pass buttonText='Save' in every version.

items.title: Skipping the body padding reset
items.detail: antd gives the drawer body 24px padding by default. Without styles={{ body: { padding: 0, display: 'flex', flexDirection: 'column' } }}, content double-pads, the footer floats mid-drawer instead of pinning to the bottom, and edge-to-edge content like tabs and grids shows a white gutter.
